problem.title: Word Pattern II
problem.description: Given a pattern and a string s, return true if s matches the pattern. A match means there is a bijection between letters in pattern and non-empty substrings in s such that each letter maps to exactly one unique substring and each substring maps to exactly one letter. Unlike Word Pattern I, words in s are not pre-split by spaces.
assumptions: Pattern letters map one-to-one to non-empty substrings (bijection).
Different pattern letters must map to different substrings.
s is not pre-split — you decide the word boundaries.

example.explanation: 'a' → 'dog', 'b' → 'cat'. Try all substring splits via backtracking. Each pattern character must consistently map to one substring with no overlap.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Backtracking [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use backtracking to try all possible ways to split s into substrings matching each character of pattern. Maintain two maps: pattern-char → substring and substring → pattern-char (to ensure bijection). At each step, either use the existing mapping for the current pattern character or try all new substrings from the current position.
